questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using subagents in Claude Code?
options: They allow tasks to be broken down and run in parallel while keeping the main context window clean
They eliminate the need for tool calls entirely
They automatically fix bugs in your codebase
They reduce the cost of API calls by half
questions.explanation: Subagents operate in isolated context windows and handle exploratory work in parallel, returning only summaries to the main context. This prevents the journey of exploration from cluttering your primary context window, which is the core benefit described. The other options misrepresent or overstate what subagents do.

questions.question: How do you create a new subagent in Claude Code?
options: Run the /agents command and select 'Create new agent'
Write Python code to instantiate a new agent class
Upload a JSON configuration file to your workspace
Use the Claude API directly with agent parameters
questions.explanation: The /agents command is the documented way to create subagents in Claude Code. Running this command walks you through steps to define scope, purpose, tools, and appearance. The other options are not supported methods for creating subagents.

questions.question: In what format are subagents defined?
options: Markdown files with YAML frontmatter
Python modules with class definitions
JSON configuration files
XML schema documents
questions.explanation: Subagents are defined in Markdown files with YAML frontmatter. This is the standard format used in Claude Code for subagent definitions. The other formats are not used for this purpose.

questions.question: When you create a subagent through the /agents command, which of the following are automatically generated?
options: Only the subagent name
The name, description, and prompt for the subagent
The name, description, prompt, and all required tools
Only the prompt and tool configuration
questions.explanation: Claude automatically generates a name, description, and prompt for the subagent when you create one through the /agents interface. The prompt helps Claude understand when to call the subagent. While you select tools during creation, they are not automatically generated—you choose them. The description and prompt are key outputs of the generation process.

questions.question: How does a subagent's context window differ from the main Claude conversation context?
options: The subagent's context is smaller and shared with other subagents
The subagent operates in its own isolated context window separate from the main conversation
The subagent's context is unlimited and never fills up
The subagent's context is identical to the main context but runs faster
questions.explanation: Each subagent operates in its own isolated context window, separate from the main Claude conversation. This isolation is what allows subagents to do exploratory work without cluttering the main context. The other options mischaracterize the relationship between subagent and main contexts.

questions.question: What is the purpose of persistent memory in subagents?
options: To store all tool call results permanently in a database
To allow the subagent to retain memory across conversations, which is useful for consistent work on the same projects
To prevent the subagent from forgetting its name and description
To cache API responses and reduce latency
questions.explanation: Persistent memory allows subagents to retain information across multiple conversations, making them effective for ongoing work on the same projects. This is particularly valuable when you use a subagent repeatedly for similar tasks. The other options describe different features or misrepresent the purpose of persistent memory.

questions.question: How do skills behave differently when loaded into a subagent compared to the main conversation?
options: Skills in subagents are limited to read-only operations
The entire skill is loaded into the subagent's context, unlike in the main conversation where only parts may be loaded
Skills cannot be used in subagents at all
Skills in subagents automatically execute without requiring Claude's approval
questions.explanation: When you preload skills into subagents, the entire skill is loaded into the subagent's context. This differs from the main conversation where only relevant portions might be loaded. This full loading ensures the subagent has complete access to the skill's functionality. The other options misrepresent how skills work in subagents.

questions.question: A developer is exploring a large codebase to understand its architecture before implementing a new feature. Why would using a subagent be beneficial in this scenario?
options: The subagent can explore the codebase in parallel and return only a summary, keeping the main context clean for feature development
The subagent can prevent the developer from making mistakes in the codebase
The subagent can automatically implement the new feature without developer input
The subagent can reduce the total time spent on the project by half
questions.explanation: This scenario directly illustrates the core benefit of subagents: they handle exploratory work (like codebase exploration) in their own context window and return only the summary findings. This prevents the exploration journey from consuming the main context window, leaving it clean for the actual feature development work. The other options either misrepresent subagent capabilities or make unsupported claims.

questions.question: When creating a subagent through the /agents command, you are asked to select tools for the subagent. What is the significance of this step?
options: It determines which tools the subagent can use to complete its assigned tasks
It automatically grants the subagent access to all tools in your workspace
It prevents the main conversation from using those tools
It ensures the subagent will never need to call the main Claude instance
questions.explanation: Selecting tools during subagent creation determines which tools that specific subagent has access to for completing its tasks. This is a customization step that tailors the subagent's capabilities to its intended purpose. The other options either overstate the effect or misrepresent how tool access works between subagents and the main conversation.

questions.question: Consider a scenario where you have multiple subagents handling different tasks (codebase exploration, web research, and documentation review). How does the subagent architecture support this multi-agent approach?
options: Each subagent shares a single context window to coordinate their work
Each subagent operates in its own isolated context window, allowing parallel execution without context interference
Only one subagent can run at a time, and they must queue their requests
Subagents automatically merge their context windows to share findings
questions.explanation: The architecture explicitly supports multiple subagents by giving each one its own isolated context window. This isolation allows them to run in parallel without their exploratory work interfering with each other or the main context. This is a key architectural advantage that enables efficient delegation of multiple tasks. The other options either contradict the parallel execution capability or misrepresent how context isolation works.

questions.question: A developer is concerned about context window efficiency. They need to run web searches for research, explore a codebase, and develop a new feature—all within the same session. How would subagents help optimize this workflow?
options: Subagents would eliminate the need for web searches and codebase exploration
Subagents would delegate the research and exploration to separate isolated contexts, returning only summaries to the main context, which remains available for feature development
Subagents would automatically combine all three tasks into a single optimized process
Subagents would require the developer to switch between different conversation windows for each task
questions.explanation: This scenario demonstrates the practical efficiency gain from subagents. By delegating research and exploration to subagents with their own context windows, the main context stays clean and available for the actual feature development work. The subagents handle the heavy lifting (web searches, codebase exploration) and return only the relevant findings, preventing context bloat. The other options either misrepresent subagent capabilities or contradict the efficiency benefits.

questions.question: When customizing a subagent with persistent memory and preloaded skills, what is the trade-off you should consider?
options: Persistent memory increases subagent cost, while preloaded skills decrease it
Persistent memory allows cross-conversation retention but may increase context usage, while preloading entire skills consumes context space but ensures complete skill access
Preloaded skills eliminate the need for persistent memory
Both features have no trade-offs and should always be enabled together
questions.explanation: Both customization features provide benefits but consume resources. Persistent memory enables the subagent to remember information across conversations, which is valuable for ongoing projects but uses storage and context space. Preloading entire skills ensures the subagent has complete access to skill functionality, but loading the entire skill into context uses context window space. Understanding this trade-off helps you decide which customizations are worth the cost for your specific use case. The other options either misrepresent the trade-offs or claim there are none.
